name: Court de Gébelin's Tarot — The Birth of the 'Book of Thoth' (1781)
description:
# Court de Gébelin's Plates (1781)

## At a glance

The hinge of the whole story. In 1781 the Protestant scholar **Antoine Court de Gébelin**
published an essay declaring the Tarot de Marseille a survival of the ancient Egyptian
**Book of Thoth**. He offered **no evidence** — and was wrong — but he converted a card
game into a "sacred text" and launched two centuries of occult tarot. Bundled with his
essay was a second piece by the **Comte de Mellet**, which first proposed *reading the
cards* and linking them to the Hebrew alphabet.

## Origin & dating

Published in volume 8 of de Gébelin's encyclopedic *Le Monde Primitif* (1781). The story
runs that he saw the tarot game being played in a Paris salon (Mme Helvétius's) and
"recognised" Egyptian allegory in the trumps. His redrawn plates "corrected" the
Marseille images toward what he imagined Egyptian originals looked like.

## Provenance & evidence

The text and plates survive and are public-domain. What does **not** exist is any
evidentiary basis: this predates Champollion's decipherment of hieroglyphs (1822), so
de Gébelin's "Egyptian" reading was pure imagination projected onto Italian Christian
imagery.

## Game or divination?

This is the **birth of the divinatory/occult claim** — but as *theory*, not practice.
De Gébelin allegorised; it was the Comte de Mellet (and then Etteilla) who turned the
idea into a method. The grammar should treat de Gébelin as the **origin of the myth**,
explicitly dated 1781, and never as evidence about the decks' actual 15th-century origin.

## The fear question

**No fear at all — the opposite.** De Gébelin published openly, under his name, in a
respectable subscription encyclopedia read by the Parisian elite (Franklin was a
subscriber). Far from hiding "occult" knowledge, he *announced* it as prestigious
ancient wisdom. The 18th-century salon treated this as fashionable erudition, not
dangerous magic. The occult overlay was, at its birth, a **public, high-status
intellectual fad**.

## Counter-voices

This file is where the "suppressed occult history" claim must be weighed most carefully.
De Gébelin is the *source* of the Egyptian-origin belief that many practitioners still
hold. Defenders argue he intuited a real esoteric substrate. The scholarly verdict
(Decker, Depaulis, Dummett) is firm: the Egyptian genealogy is **invented**, not
recovered — a Romantic-Enlightenment fantasy about Egypt, not a transmission from it.
Importantly, debunking de Gébelin does **not** debunk the *real* later history of
divinatory practice or its persecuted practitioners; it only removes a false pedigree.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Clearly separate de Gébelin's essay from Mellet's appended essay — they differ (e.g.
  trump-to-Hebrew-letter assignments).
- The plates are interpretive redraws; flag them as such, not as historical decks.

## Sources & further reading

- "Antoine Court de Gébelin," Wikipedia; "De Gébelin's Tarot Essay in Le Monde Primitif,"
  Mary K. Greer.
- Decker, Depaulis & Dummett, *A Wicked Pack of Cards* (1996).

---
*Research notes — AI-assisted first-pass draft, pending review by the maintainer and the Tarot History Forum. Skeptical, document-first (Dummett spine). Corrections welcome via pending edit.*

**RESEARCH NOTE (2026-06-13).** Two etymologies are often blurred: the **'Royal Road' (Tar + Ro)** is **Gébelin's**; the **'Ta-Rosh = road/science of Thoth'** is the **Comte de Mellet's**. Gébelin's birth year is contested across sources (1719/1724/1725/1728). The Wheel's Sphinx/Typhon/Hermanubis figures are later Lévi/Waite additions, NOT in Gébelin's 1781 plate. See research/decks/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md.

sections.Iconography: A traveller in motley with a staff and bundle, a beast at his leg — the Marseille Fool, copied into the plate.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Court de Gébelin reads the unnumbered card as Folly (la Folie) — the senseless man, outside the sacred sequence.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** Same image; the change is conceptual — the Marseille's nameless wandering jester is fixed as **"zero,"** the head of an alphabet-of-22, giving the Fool the structural prominence every later occult deck grants it [@web_trionfi_gebelin]. 

*Sources:* [@trionfi], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_tarotforum_mellet], [@web_trionfi_gebelin].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A figure in a jester's costume walks with a bundle on a stick, a small animal at his heels. The plate is an engraving based on a Marseille-type deck, rendered in de Gébelin's archaeological style.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin identified this card with Typhon or the god of chaos in his Egyptian reading of the tarot.

sections.Iconography: A figure at a table of cups, coins and knives, wand raised.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: He reads the Bateleur as the active, material principle — the conjuror, the maker, the first agent of creation.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille's street-performer *bateleur* is elevated from fairground conjuror to a quasi-priestly figure of cosmic agency, his cups/coins/swords/baton turned into element-symbols — the first step of the long transformation of this card into the occult "Magician" [@web_robertplace_firstoccult]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_villarevak_gebelin].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A standing figure at a table spread with various small objects and tools. He wears a wide-brimmed hat and gestures with a wand. The engraving simplifies the Marseille Bateleur.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin called this card the 'Juggler,' the first principle of magic and skill in the Egyptian Book of Thoth narrative.

sections.Iconography: A veiled, enthroned woman in a tiara with a book.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: For Court de Gébelin she is the HIGH PRIESTESS of Egypt — sacred wisdom in female form, not a scandalous 'popess'.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille *Papesse* — an awkward Christian image (a female pope) often explained away as Pope Joan or a heresy — is re-titled and re-sexed into **Egyptian priestess-goddess**, removing the Christian embarrassment and making the card the keystone of the Isis reading [@web_traditionaltarot_isis]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A seated woman in a veil and headdress, holding a book. She wears a papal tiara, shown enthroned frontally. The plate follows the Marseille Papesse closely.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin identified her as Isis, the keeper of sacred knowledge, rather than a Christian Papess.

sections.Iconography: A crowned woman with sceptre and eagle shield.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Queen, or the female sovereign principle of the Egyptian state and nature's fertility.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** Modest — the feudal Empress is folded into the Egyptian royal frame as Osiris's counterpart; the change is the *context* (Egyptian sovereignty) rather than the image (confidence: medium — Gébelin's text is least explicit here). 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A winged empress is shown seated on a throne, holding a sceptre, with an eagle-bearing shield at her side. Her crown and wings appear in the engraved version.
sections.Symbol: Identified by de Gébelin as Isis in her role as queen of heaven and guardian of the natural world.

sections.Iconography: A bearded sovereign in profile with sceptre and shield.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The King — temporal sovereignty among the Egyptians.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Holy Roman Emperor becomes an **Egyptian** king-god (Osiris), anchoring the Isis/Osiris/Typhon family Gébelin scatters across the trumps [@web_traditionaltarot_isis]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A crowned emperor sits on a throne in profile, holding a sceptre and orb. The plate renders the standard Marseille Emperor in engraved line.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin identified this figure with Osiris, king of Egypt and lord of the living.

sections.Iconography: A tiara'd pontiff blessing two acolytes.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Court de Gébelin makes him the GRAND HIEROPHANT — the chief priest of the Egyptian mysteries.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Catholic Pope is de-Christianised into the **pagan high priest of the mysteries**, exactly paralleling the Papess→Isis-priestess move; this is where the modern title "Hierophant" originates [@web_robertplace_firstoccult]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_villarevak_gebelin].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A hierophant or pope is enthroned between two pillars, raising his hand in blessing toward two kneeling figures. He wears a triple crown and holds a triple cross.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin associated this card with the Egyptian high priest, the intermediary between gods and humanity.

sections.Iconography: A youth between two women beneath a winged Cupid drawing his bow.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Court de Gébelin reads it as **Marriage** (his caption for the card). The famous moral reading — the youth choosing between **Vice and Virtue**, Hercules at the crossroads — is the **Comte de Mellet's** contribution in the same 1781 volume, not Gébelin's. [Correction 2026-06-13; src: R. M. Place, benebell wen]
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille's love-triangle-under-Cupid is first narrowed to **"Marriage"** (Gébelin), then moralised into the **Choice / Vice-and-Virtue** allegory (Mellet) that later cartomancers inherited — though the earliest cards show simple love, not moral choice [@web_benebell_lovers]. *Correction to existing grammar: the Vice/Virtue–Hercules reading is Mellet's, not Gébelin's.*. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_lovers], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A young man stands between two women while a winged Cupid aims an arrow from above. The engraving follows the Marseille 'Amoureux' composition.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin called this card the 'Marriage' of Osiris and Isis, or the union of opposing principles.

sections.Iconography: A crowned prince in a canopied chariot drawn by two horses.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Osiris triumphant, or the King in his war-chariot — victory and dominion.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The generic Renaissance triumph-chariot is given an **Egyptian royal/Osirian** gloss; image unchanged, frame Egyptianised (confidence: medium on the explicit "Osiris" label for this specific card). 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_villarevak_gebelin].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A crowned warrior in a canopied chariot is drawn by two horses. He holds a sceptre. The Marseille chariot composition is reproduced faithfully in the engraving.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin identified this as Osiris triumphant, returning victorious from a military campaign.

sections.Iconography: An enthroned woman with sword and balance.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: One of the three cardinal Virtues the Egyptians set among the sacred cards.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** Little visual change; Gébelin gathers Justice (VIII), Strength (XI), Temperance (XIIII) and the re-read Hanged Man as Prudence (XII) into the **complete set of four cardinal Virtues**, presenting their presence as proof of a designed wisdom-system [@web_benebell_lovers]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_lovers], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A crowned woman sits on a throne holding a sword upright and scales in the other hand, in the standard Marseille Justice pose.
sections.Symbol: Justice identified with Ma'at, the Egyptian goddess of truth, balance, and cosmic order.

sections.Iconography: A cloaked old man with a staff and a shielded lantern.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Sage or Hermit seeking truth — Court de Gébelin reads the lantern-bearer as **Diogenes**, searching by daylight for an honest man (i.e. for virtue). (Earlier note's 'Capuchin/Egyptian seeker' was unsupported.) [Correction 2026-06-13]
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille's *Hermite* (sometimes read as Father Time / Old Age) is recast as the **searching philosopher (Diogenes)** — a wisdom-seeker rather than a figure of mortality [@web_robertplace_firstoccult]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: An elder in a long robe walks hunched over a staff, holding a lantern before him. The Hermit is shown in profile moving across a blank ground.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin interpreted this card as the 'Sage' — the Egyptian philosopher who carries the lamp of wisdom through darkness.

items.name: 10 — Wheel of Fortune (La Roue de Fortune)

illustrations.scene: Wheel of Fortune (La Roue de Fortune)

sections.Iconography: A wheel turned by figures rising and falling.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The wheel of destiny — the turning of fortune the Egyptian priests taught.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** Essentially unchanged in meaning; **note for accuracy** — the later Sphinx-couchant / Typhon-descending / Hermanubis-rising figures belong to **Lévi and Waite's** reconstructions, **not** Gébelin's plate, which keeps the Marseille's nondescript beasts. Do not import them into the 1781 reading [@web_benebell_lovers]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_lovers], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_villarevak_gebelin].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A spoked wheel at the card's centre turns while figures cling to its rim at various positions. A sphinx or animal figure sits atop the wheel.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin associated the Wheel with the Egyptian cycle of the Nile flood — the year's turning and the alternation of abundance and scarcity.

sections.Iconography: A woman calmly parting a lion's jaws.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Fortitude — the second of the three cardinal Virtues among the sacred cards.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** Image and meaning essentially as in the Marseille; the change is its enrolment in Gébelin's four-Virtues scheme [@web_benebell_lovers]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_lovers], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A woman gently holds open the jaws of a lion. The standard Marseille Strength composition is reproduced in the engraving.
sections.Symbol: Physical force tamed by moral virtue; de Gébelin read this as the Egyptian virtue of courage.

sections.Iconography: A youth suspended by one foot from a frame.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Court de Gébelin believed the card was printed UPSIDE-DOWN: rightly turned, he says, it shows Prudence, the fourth cardinal Virtue, with a raised foot.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** A genuine **visual** change, not just an interpretive one: the figure is flipped so that punishment/suspension becomes **deliberation/Prudence**. This is the clearest case of a plate "corrected" to fit the thesis [@web_researchgate_prudence]. 

*Sources:* [@web_alamy_gebelin], [@web_researchgate_prudence], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A man hangs inverted by one ankle from a gallows, his free leg crossed behind, his arms relaxed at his sides.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin erroneously interpreted this card as the 'Prudent Man,' suggesting a figure in contemplation rather than punishment or sacrifice.

sections.Iconography: A skeleton with a scythe among the fallen — the card left unnamed in the Marseille.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Death — the great Egyptian passage; he notes the Marseille leaves it nameless out of dread.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille's deliberately **anonymous** trump XIII is named outright as Death and folded into an Egyptian discourse on mortality and the unlucky number [@web_benebell_lovers]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_lovers], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A skeleton with a great scythe harvests a field strewn with severed body parts. The plate is an engraved version of the unnamed Death card.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin saw this as Typhon, the Egyptian destroyer, moving through creation with no regard for rank.

sections.Iconography: A winged figure pouring liquid between two vessels.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: Temperance — the third of the cardinal Virtues set among the sacred cards.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** Unchanged in image and meaning; enrolled in the four-Virtues scheme [@web_benebell_lovers]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_lovers], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A winged figure pours liquid between two vessels, standing in the standard Marseille Temperance pose.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin identified this as Temperance in the Pythagorean sense: harmony and measured proportion.

sections.Iconography: A horned, winged figure over two bound captives.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: TYPHON — the evil principle of Egyptian theology, the destroyer set against Osiris.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Christian **Devil** is swapped for the Egyptian **Typhon (Set)**, completing the Osiris/Isis/Typhon triad and de-Christianising the card's evil principle [@web_traditionaltarot_isis]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis], [@web_villarevak_gebelin].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A horned figure on a pedestal with two smaller creatures chained at its feet. The plate renders the Marseille Devil in simplified engraved form.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin associated this card with Typhon-Set, the Egyptian embodiment of chaos and the principle of destruction.

sections.Iconography: Figures fall from a tower struck by fire from the sky.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: He reads the 'Maison-Dieu' as the Castle of Plutus, or the temple of pride struck down — calamity and the fall of avarice.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille's *Maison-Dieu* is glossed as the **Castle of Plutus / fall of avarice**, an allegory of toppled wealth rather than a literal lightning-struck tower [@web_traditionaltarot_isis]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A tower on high ground is struck by fire or lightning; its top is blasted off and two figures fall from its height.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin called this card 'La Maison-Dieu' (House of God) and interpreted it as the destruction of the Tower of Babel.

sections.Iconography: A naked woman kneels pouring two urns into water and onto land, beneath a great star and seven small ones.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: His most famous reading: the great star is SIRIUS (Sothis), the Dog-Star whose rising herald the flooding of the NILE; the woman pours the inundation that renews Egypt.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille's generic star-and-bathing-woman becomes a precise **Egyptian astronomical calendar image** — Sirius + Nile flood + Isis — the most fully "Egyptianised" card in the essay [@web_traditionaltarot_isis]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis], [@web_traditionaltarot_star].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A kneeling figure pours water from two pitchers near a pool. Above, a large star is flanked by seven smaller stars.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin identified this card with the heliacal rising of Sirius (the Dog Star), which heralded the Nile flood and the new year in Egypt.

sections.Iconography: A dripping moon over two dogs and a crayfish between two towers.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Moon and its dew; he reads the falling drops as the influence of the moon on the Egyptian year.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille's eerie moonscape is decoded as an **Egyptian astronomical/agricultural** allegory (tropics-guardians, Cancer, the flood) rather than a scene of night-fears [@web_traditionaltarot_isis]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis], [@web_wopc_mondeprimitif].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A crescent moon hangs above a landscape with two towers, two dogs, and a crayfish emerging from water.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin saw this as the Moon in its Egyptian role as timekeeper, marking the month and guiding nocturnal navigation.

sections.Iconography: A radiant sun over two children by a wall.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Sun — Osiris as the day-star, the source of Egyptian life.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** Image as in the Marseille; the gloss makes it an **Egyptian solar** emblem (and supplies the "tears of gold and pearl" phrase later writers reused) [@web_traditionaltarot_isis]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis], [@web_villarevak_gebelin].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A radiant sun shines over two children in a garden. The standard Marseille Sun plate is reproduced in engraving.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin identified this as the sun god Ra, or Apollo — the source of all light, growth, and civilisation.

sections.Iconography: An angel trumpets while the dead rise from a tomb.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: The Creation, or the Last Judgement — the awakening of the dead the Egyptians taught.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Christian **Last Judgement** is flipped into its opposite — a scene of **Creation** — to fit an Egyptian-cosmogony reading [@web_robertplace_firstoccult]. 

*Sources:* [@web_benebell_lovers], [@web_robertplace_firstoccult].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: An angel blows a trumpet from clouds above while three figures rise from tombs.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin interpreted this as Judgment Day in the Christian overlay but noted its Egyptian precedent in the resurrection of Osiris.

sections.Iconography: A dancing figure in a wreath, the four beasts in the corners.
sections.Court de Gébelin's Egyptian Reading: TIME, or the Universe — Court de Gébelin reads the central figure as Isis and the four beasts as the four seasons/elements: the whole created world.
sections.Tradition Note: Court de Gébelin's claim (1781) that the tarot is an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth' is a myth — the tarot is a 15th-century Italian card game. But this essay INVENTED esoteric tarot: every occult deck and the whole practice of tarot divination descend from this single, mistaken, fertile idea.
sections.Research note:
**What changed (vs. its parent):** The Marseille's *Monde* (a dancer in a mandorla with the four evangelist-beasts) is re-identified as **Isis / Time / the Universe**, the four Christian living-creatures becoming the **four seasons** — the capstone of the Egyptian reading and Mellet's Golden-Age starting point [@web_traditionaltarot_isis]. 

*Sources:* [@web_robertplace_firstoccult], [@web_traditionaltarot_isis], [@web_wopc_mondeprimitif].

*Full card-by-card research:* `research/cards/court-de-gebelin-tarot.md`.
sections.Scene: A dancer at the centre of a laurel wreath, with the four tetramorphs at the corners. The Marseille World composition is reproduced as an engraved plate.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin called this card 'Le Monde' and associated it with the completion of time, the universal frame holding all creation.

items.name: The 'Book of Thoth' — The Birth of Occult Tarot

sections.What happened here: In 1781 Antoine Court de Gébelin looked at an ordinary Tarot de Marseille — a card game — and declared it the last survivor of an ancient Egyptian 'Book of Thoth', a hieroglyphic encyclopaedia of priestly wisdom carried west by the 'Gypsies'. He was wrong on every count: tarot is a 15th-century Italian game, the 'Egyptian' reading is invented, and the Romani have no link to Egypt. Hieroglyphs would not be deciphered for another 40 years.
sections.Why it matters: And yet this single essay created esoteric tarot. Within a decade Etteilla built the first divination deck on it; Lévi added the Kabbalah; Wirth, the Golden Dawn, Waite, and Crowley followed. Every occult tarot — and the whole idea that tarot tells fortunes — descends from this beautiful mistake. The companion essay by the Comte de Mellet, in the same volume, first proposed the Hebrew-letter associations and the reverse-order Egyptian cosmogony.
sections.Scene: The 22 atout plates from Antoine Court de Gébelin's 'Le Monde Primitif' (1781), Volume VIII. Each plate is a rough line engraving based on a Tarot de Marseille deck, accompanied in the text by de Gébelin's elaborate theory that the tarot is the surviving fragments of the Egyptian Book of Thoth.
sections.Symbol: De Gébelin's plates inaugurated the occult tarot tradition. His Egyptian identification — later refuted by Egyptology — proved enormously influential, launching two centuries of esoteric tarot commentary. The plates themselves are historically important as the first published scholarly reproduction of tarot cards.
